metric_value: CGI scripts; poor scaling with large users/transactions/databases; little flexibility or system integration
source_page: What is a Dynamic Web Application?
notes: Aberdeen's three-tier market classification; low-end covers basic catalog servers using CGI.

metric_value: ODBC/native drivers with SQL-like query; basic cross-sell/up-sell business rules; improved database access
source_page: What is a Dynamic Web Application?
notes: Mid-range tier serves smaller companies for catalog-specific requirements but fails large intranet/extranet production criteria.

metric_value: Object-oriented middleware foundation with TP-monitor technology; maximum scalability, performance, system integration
source_page: What is a Dynamic Web Application?
notes: Aberdeen identifies CORBA-based OO middleware as the key to high-end tier's ability to scale and integrate.

metric_name: Dynamic page generation performance - Custom CGI baseline
metric_value: 4,737 pages per hour (custom CGI on single-processor Sun UltraSPARC)
notes: Source: BroadVision, Inc. benchmark data.

metric_name: Dynamic page generation performance - Custom NSAPI
metric_value: 5,455 pages per hour (custom NSAPI on single-processor Sun UltraSPARC)
notes: Source: BroadVision, Inc. benchmark data. ~15% improvement over custom CGI.

metric_name: Dynamic page generation performance - One-to-One with CGI
metric_value: 21,176 pages per hour (One-to-One with CGI on single-processor Sun UltraSPARC)
notes: Source: BroadVision, Inc. 4.5x improvement over custom CGI.

metric_name: Dynamic page generation performance - One-to-One with NSAPI
metric_value: 51,428 pages per hour (One-to-One with NSAPI on single-processor Sun UltraSPARC); ~9,990% improvement vs. Custom CGI
notes: Source: BroadVision, Inc. benchmark data. One-to-One with NSAPI shows 140% improvement over One-to-One with CGI.

metric_value: Interfaces with approximately 30 mainframe systems, customer/job scheduling apps, inventory management, telephone and billing systems
notes: USWest extranet implemented to meet FCC 1996 Telecommunications Act interconnection requirements for competitive carriers.

metric_name: USWest development time savings from One-to-One
metric_value: USWest bought 'at least six months of development time as well as linear scalability' vs. building in-house
notes: USWest officials stated this directly to Aberdeen; validates rapid ROI claim for One-to-One.

metric_name: Internet customers as % of total sales in conventional catalog industries
metric_value: 1-2% of total sales from new online customers in most large conventional catalog industries
notes: Aberdeen's estimate of incremental online revenue at time of study; e-commerce was in very early growth phase.

metric_name: Web-based customer service cost vs. call center
metric_value: Web-based customer service costs an order of magnitude less than call center at $2 per call
notes: UPS, FedEx cited as realizing millions in savings from web-based customer service. This cost differential has proven persistent.

metric_name: Customer acquisition cost vs. retention cost
metric_value: Cost of securing new customer is ten times as great as selling to existing customer
notes: Classic CRM economic rationale; still widely cited in marketing analytics; drives cross-sell/up-sell strategy.

metric_value: Aberdeen strongly recommends all Fortune-1000 corporate web site buyers evaluate BroadVision; predicts rest of industry will choose similar relationship management strategy
notes: Aberdeen's strong endorsement; 'Aberdeen strongly recommends that all potential buyers of software for Fortune-1000 corporate Web sites evaluate BroadVision's software.'

metric_value: BroadVision stock peaked at $26B market cap in 2000; crashed in dot-com bust; nearly delisted; Salesforce.com (founded 1999) captured CRM market; BroadVision became marginal player; acquired by ESW Capital (private equity) May 2020
notes: Source: BroadVision Wikipedia; BroadVision Group about-us page. BroadVision pioneered the market but did not maintain leadership; Salesforce, SAP, and Oracle dominated CRM.

metric_name: Smart card adoption for retail personalization
metric_value: Smart cards will allow financial institutions to bridge internet storefronts and brick-and-mortar stores; enable cross-pollination of promotions
notes: Aberdeen predicted smart cards would be the bridge for online-offline customer data; this occurred but differently (NFC/contactless, EMV) not the BroadVision-centric vision.

metric_value: Smart card (EMV chip) became universal by 2015 for payment security but did not bridge online/offline personalization as Aberdeen predicted; that role was filled by loyalty programs, cookies, and mobile apps
notes: EMV chip cards achieved wide adoption for security, not marketing personalization as Aberdeen envisioned.

metric_value: Push technologies merely shift where information resides and do not significantly improve worker productivity or personalize end-user interaction
source_page: Relationship Management: Avoiding A Technology Morass
notes: Aberdeen dismisses PointCast-style push as 'automated pull' that merely categorizes information users will be 'swamped' with.

metric_value: PointCast declined and was sold to entrypoint.com in 1999 for approximately $7M after failing to achieve $400M expected IPO; push technology category disappeared by 2000
notes: Aberdeen's assessment of push technology limitations proved accurate; PointCast became the canonical example of overhyped internet technology.

metric_name: Customer data as competitive advantage prediction
metric_value: Consumer (end-user) data is the competitive advantage; serious internet businesses in financial services and computer sales already recognize this
notes: Aberdeen's core thesis that customer data ownership is the strategic battleground of internet commerce.

metric_name: Customer data competitive advantage actual outcome
metric_value: Customer data became the defining competitive advantage of the internet era; data-driven personalization (Netflix, Amazon, Google) confirmed Aberdeen's prediction; market for CRM and CDP reached $50B+ by 2020
notes: Aberdeen's 1997 prediction about customer data as competitive advantage proved remarkably prescient; entire internet economy organized around this principle.

metric_value: Three differentiators: (1) integrated multi-tech system; (2) management/dev tools matching user skills; (3) industry-leading user profile management for custom interaction
notes: Aberdeen identifies BroadVision's three-front competitive differentiation from IBM, Oracle, Microsoft, Interworld.

metric_value: CORBA-based system scales by replacing or adding servers; communicates with existing apps via screen scraping or other CORBA systems
notes: CORBA compliance enables integration with IBM and Oracle CORBA-based systems via lower-level objects.

metric_name: Internet ROI timeline for large catalog vendors
metric_value: Return on investment achievable in as little as six months with third-party solutions per large internet catalog vendors
notes: Aberdeen interviewed large internet computer catalog vendors who cited 6-month ROI timeframe; Aberdeen believes faster ROI with One-to-One.

metric_value: Aberdeen believes BroadVision is NOT too early for the market; serious internet businesses already recognize customer data as competitive advantage
notes: Aberdeen addresses skeptics who might think personalized web applications are premature.

metric_value: Interaction manager maintains persistent connections; caches user profiles, content, categories; amortizes database cost over multiple sessions
notes: Database connections are primary bottleneck (~10x cost of other operations); BroadVision's connection pooling/caching addresses this.
